16: ouketi hos doulon alla hyper doulon adelphon agapeton (ouketi hōs doulon alla hyper doulon, adelphon agapēton): 'no longer as a slave but more than a slave, as a dear brother.' The grammatical structure — ouketi ... alla hyper (no longer ... but beyond) — expresses a qualitative transformation, not just a label change. Onesimus remains in a social relationship with Philemon but that relationship is now defined by a different primary category: adelphos agapētos (beloved brother). Paul does not directly command emancipation, but the logic of brotherhood makes slavery an anomaly: how can you own your brother? The letter is the most politically charged use of household-code language in Paul — Christian kinship (adelphos) is placed in deliberate tension with Roman social institution (doulos).
18: ei de ti edikesen se e opheilei touto emoi ellogei (ei de ti ēdikēsen se ē opheilei, touto emoi ellogei): 'If he has wronged you at all or owes you anything, charge that to my account.' Ellogei (charge to my account / impute) is a commercial-accounting term used only here and Romans 5:13 (sin is not ellogeitai when there is no law) in the NT. Paul offers to absorb Onesimus's debt as a surety. The theological parallel is explicit in the letter's rhetoric: as Paul stands in for Onesimus absorbing his debt-liability, Christ stands in for sinners absorbing their debt before God. Philemon is the NT's most compressed illustration of imputed righteousness in a human-relational register.
1: Paulos desmios Christou Iēsou (Paul, prisoner of Christ Jesus): the self-designation desmios (prisoner, one in chains) replaces the standard Pauline apostolic title. Paul does not claim apostolic authority at the outset — instead his imprisonment becomes the basis of his appeal (Philemon 1:9-10). Christou Iēsou qualifies the imprisonment: Paul is not merely Rome's prisoner but Christ's — his chains are in service of Christ rather than the result of crime. Timotheos ho adelphos (Timothy our brother) — the co-sender lends weight to the appeal without supplanting Paul's personal voice; the letter remains first-person singular throughout despite the plural opening.
2: Apphia tē adelphē (Apphia our sister): the feminine name is Phrygian in origin; she is likely Philemon's wife given her placement between him and Archippus. Archippō tō systratiōtē hēmōn (Archippus our fellow soldier) — the military metaphor for ministry-partnership (systratiōtēs) appears also in Philippians 2:25 for Epaphroditus. Archippus may be the Colossian minister of Colossians 4:17. Kata oikon sou ekklēsia (the church in your house) — the household church as the basic social unit of early Christianity; Philemon's home is both his private dwelling and the assembly point of the local church. This means Onesimus's return will be a public, community event.
3: Charis hymin kai eirēnē apo theou patros hēmōn kai Kyriou Iēsou Christou (grace and peace to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ): the standard Pauline greeting formula. The plural hymin (to you, plural) addresses the entire community gathered in Philemon's house (Philemon 1:2), not only Philemon himself. Paul's appeal is thus conducted not privately but in the sight of the community — a subtle social pressure that the household church will be watching how Philemon responds to the letter.
4: Eucharisteō tō theō mou pantote mneian sou poioumenos epi tōn proseuchōn mou (I thank my God always, making mention of you in my prayers): the thanksgiving section of Philemon is among the most personal in the Pauline corpus. Pantote (always) — continuous, habitual prayer for Philemon. Mneian sou poioumenos (making mention of you) — mneia (mention, memory, remembrance) at the prayers: Philemon occupies a standing place in Paul's prayer life. The thanksgiving primes Philemon emotionally before the appeal of Philemon 1:8-21.
5: Akouōn sou tēn agapēn kai tēn pistin hēn echeis pros ton Kyrion Iēsoun kai eis pantas tous hagious (hearing of your love and faith which you have toward the Lord Jesus and for all the saints): the chiastic structure of Philemon 1:5 — love and faith / toward the Lord / and toward all the saints — places the Godward and humanward expressions of Philemon's character in view simultaneously. The report (akouōn, hearing) has reached Paul in prison — Philemon's reputation precedes him. The appeal of Philemon 1:9 (on the basis of love) takes up this established character: Paul appeals to a quality already known to exist.
6: Hopōs hē koinōnia tēs pisteōs sou energēs genētai en epignōsei pantos agathou tou en hēmin eis Christon (so that the partnership/sharing of your faith may become effective in the knowledge of every good thing that is in us toward Christ): the most grammatically compressed verse in the letter and among the most disputed in the Pauline corpus. Koinōnia tēs pisteōs — the participation that faith creates; the shared-life that Christian trust generates. Energēs (effective, active, at work) — from ergon (work); faith-koinōnia that produces real effects. Epignōsis (full knowledge/recognition) — the precise knowledge that is the fruit of active faith; the prayer is that Philemon's effective faith-partnership will deepen his comprehensive recognition of every good thing 'in us toward Christ'
7: Charan gar pollēn eschon kai paraklēsin epi tē agapē sou, hoti ta splanchna tōn hagiōn anapepautai dia sou, adelphe (for I have had much joy and encouragement from your love, because the hearts of the saints have been refreshed through you, brother): splanchna (bowels, viscera — the seat of deep emotion in antiquity; our closest equivalent is "heart" or "gut"): Philemon has refreshed the splanchna of the saints — the word recurs in Philemon 1:12 (Onesimus is Paul's own splanchna) and Philemon 1:20 (Paul asks for splanchna-refreshment from Philemon). Anapepautai (has been rested/refreshed, perfect passive) — the completed refreshment still in effect. The direct address adelphe (brother) is unusually warm for a Pauline letter opening.
8: Dio, pollēn en Christō parrēsian echōn epitassein soi to anēkon (therefore, although I have great boldness in Christ to order you what is fitting): parrēsia (boldness, frank speech, freedom of expression) — the Greco-Roman philosophical and political virtue of speaking freely and honestly; here Paul's apostolic authority grounds his parrēsia. Epitassein (to command, order) — the vocabulary of apostolic authority; Paul has the standing to issue a command. The conditional construction — 'although I have this authority' — is a rhetorical captatio: Paul invokes the authority he is choosing not to exercise, making the appeal more effective than a direct command.
9: Dia tēn agapēn mallon parakalō, toioutos ōn hōs Paulos presbyteros, nyni de kai desmios Christou Iēsou (rather I appeal on account of love, being such a one as Paul — an old man and now also a prisoner of Christ Jesus): presbyteros (old man) — the natural reading of the MSS; J. B. Lightfoot's emendation to presbeutēs (ambassador) has some appeal but lacks manuscript support. Paul as "old man" appeals to pathos: the aged prisoner asking a favor. The pairing of age (presbyteros) and imprisonment (desmios) makes the appeal from weakness rather than authority — the rhetorical inverse of Philemon 1:8's rejected command.
10: Parakalō se peri tou emou teknou, hon egennēsa en tois desmois Onēsimon (I appeal to you concerning my child Onesimus, whom I fathered in my imprisonment): only at Philemon 1:10 does Paul name Onesimus — a deliberate rhetorical delay. Having established love (Philemon 1:4-7), authority yielded for love (Philemon 1:8-9), and Paul's own situation as prisoner-old-man (Philemon 1:9), the name arrives with maximum emotional weight. Egennēsa en tois desmois (I fathered/begat in my chains) — the spiritual birth language (gennaō) of 1 Corinthians 4:15 and Galatians 4:19; Paul is Onesimus's spiritual father, making Onesimus his son (teknou).
11: Ton pote soi achrēston, nyn de soi kai emoi euchrēston (who was formerly useless to you but now is useful both to you and to me): the wordplay on the name Onesimus is the letter's central pun. Onēsimos means "useful/profitable" in Greek (from oninēmi, to benefit). Achrēstos (useless) and euchrēstos (useful) contain a further resonance: achristos (without Christ) vs. euchrēstos / in Christ — the sounds are close enough in Greek to hear the Christological overtone. The pre-conversion Onesimus was both "useless" and "christless"; the converted Onesimus is both "useful" and "in Christ." The letter invites Philemon to hear both registers simultaneously.
12: Hon anapempsa soi, auton, touto estin ta ema splanchna (I am sending him back to you — him who is my very heart/bowels): the double naming — auton (him) + ta ema splanchna (my very bowels/heart) — emphasizes the personal cost of the return. Paul sends back his own heart. Splanchna recurs from Philemon 1:7 (Philemon refreshed the saints' hearts); the emotional vocabulary creates a chain: Philemon's love has refreshed many hearts (Philemon 1:7), now Paul sends his own heart to Philemon (Philemon 1:12), and asks Philemon to refresh Paul's heart (Philemon 1:20).
13: Hon egō eboulomēn pros emauton katechein, hina hyper sou moi diakona en tois desmois tou euangeliou (whom I would have liked to keep with me, so that he might serve me in your place in my imprisonment for the gospel): the subjunctive eboulomēn (I would have wished) describes the counterfactual — what Paul could have done but chose not to. Hyper sou (on your behalf, in your place) — Onesimus as Philemon's proxy-minister to the imprisoned Paul; Philemon's generosity extended to Paul through Onesimus. This constructs the return of Onesimus not as restoration of a slave but as Philemon's opportunity to serve Paul through his representative.
14: Chōris de tēs sēs gnōmēs ouden ēthelēsa poiēsai, hina mē hōs kata anankēn to agathon sou ē alla kata hekousion (but without your consent I wanted to do nothing, so that your good deed might not be as under compulsion but voluntary): gnōmē (judgment, opinion, consent) — Paul respects Philemon's autonomy by not acting without his agreement. Anankē (compulsion, necessity) vs. hekousios (voluntary, willing) — the forced gift vs. the free gift; compelled generosity has no moral value. Paul's entire rhetorical strategy of persuasion rather than command (Philemon 1:8-9) serves this principle: he wants Philemon's free choice, not legal compliance.
15: Tacha gar dia touto echōristhē pros hōran, hina aiōnion auton apechēs (perhaps for this reason he was separated for a while, so that you might have him back forever): echōristhē — the divine passive; not "he ran away" or "he absconded" but "he was separated" — the providential framing that sees Onesimus's departure as divinely orchestrated rather than merely criminal. Pros hōran (for an hour/a while) vs. aiōnion (forever/eternally) — the contrast of temporal and eternal duration; the brief separation serves an eternal reunion. This is the letter's most explicit theological reframing of the situation.
17: Ei oun me echeis koinōnon, proslabou auton hōs eme (if then you consider me a partner, receive him as you would receive me): koinōnos (partner, sharer) — one who shares in the same enterprise; Paul's appeal reaches its crux: the logic of gospel-partnership (koinōnia) requires receiving Onesimus as one receives Paul. Proslabou (receive, take to yourself, welcome) — the verb of welcoming into one's fellowship. The argument is elegant: if Philemon and Paul are koinōnoi (partners) in the faith, and Onesimus is now Paul's son (Philemon 1:10) and Paul's very heart (Philemon 1:12), then receiving Onesimus is receiving Paul's heart into the partnership.
19: Egō Paulos egrapsa tē emē cheiri, egō apotisō (I, Paul, write with my own hand — I will repay it): the shift to autograph (egrapsa tē emē cheiri) signals a legally significant act; in the ancient world an autograph note functioned as a binding IOU. The emphatic first-person — egō Paulos (I, Paul) — and the present autograph (egō egrapsa) make this a legal pledge. Apotisō (I will repay/reimburse) — the commercial-legal verb for financial restitution. Hina mē legō soi hoti kai seauton moi prosopheileis (not to say that you owe me your very self) — the witty parenthesis that undoes the commercial logic: Paul writes an IOU but then notes that the debt runs the other way; Philemon owes Paul his spiritual life.
20: Nai, adelphe, egō sou onaimēn en Kyriō (yes, brother, let me benefit from you in the Lord): onaimēn (optative of oninēmi, to benefit/profit) — a second Onesimus-wordplay: Paul uses the verb cognate to the name Onesimus. Just as the name Onesimus means "useful/profitable," Paul asks Philemon to let him have onēsin (benefit, profit) from Philemon through Onesimus's reception. Anapausson mou ta splanchna en Christō (refresh my heart/bowels in Christ) — the third and final splanchna in the letter (Philemon 1:7, 12, 20); the chain closes: Philemon has refreshed others' hearts (Philemon 1:7); Paul sent his own heart in Onesimus (Philemon 1:12); now Paul asks Philemon to refresh Paul's heart (Philemon 1:20).
21: Pepoithōs tē hypakoē sou egrapsa soi, eidōs hoti kai hyper ha legō poiēseis (confident of your obedience I write to you, knowing that you will do even more than I say): pepoithōs (confident, trusting — perfect participle of peithō) — the letter closes as it opened, with confidence in Philemon's character. Hypakoē (obedience) — the same word used for the community's response to the gospel (Romans 1:5; Philemon 6:16); here Philemon's response to the apostolic appeal. Kai hyper ha legō poiēseis (you will do even more than I say) — the final nudge: Paul does not explicitly ask for manumission (freedom for Onesimus) but implies it; "more than I say" is the coded request for the freedom that Paul has been gesturing toward throughout.
22: Hama de kai hetoimaze moi xeniaan, elipzō gar hoti dia tōn proseuchōn hymōn charisthēsomai hymin (at the same time prepare a guest room for me, for I hope that through your prayers I will be given back to you): xenia (hospitality, guest room, lodging) — the apostle's request for accommodation signals his expectation of release and visit. Dia tōn proseuchōn hymōn (through your prayers) — the plural hymōn addresses the household church again; the community's prayers participate in Paul's hoped-for release. Charisthēsomai (I will be given as a grace/gift) — the divine passive of gracious giving; Paul's release will be a divine gift mediated through the community's intercession.
23: Aspazetai se Epaphras ho synaichmalōtos mou en Christō Iēsou (Epaphras my fellow prisoner in Christ Jesus greets you): Epaphras appears in Colossians 1:7 and 4:12 as the founder of the Colossian church and Paul's "faithful minister of Christ." Synaichmalōtos (fellow prisoner of war) — the compound of syn (with) + aichmalōtos (prisoner of war, captured in battle); a stronger word than desmios (bound prisoner). Whether Epaphras was literally co-imprisoned with Paul or the term is metaphorical (prisoner of Christ, as in Romans 7:23) is disputed — Colossians 4:10 uses it for Aristarchus.
24: Markos, Aristarchos, Dēmas, Loukas, hoi synergoi mou (Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, Luke, my fellow workers): the collegial list of Paul's companions in Rome. Mark (John Mark) had been the point of contention between Paul and Barnabas (Acts 15:37-39); his restoration to Paul's circle is complete (cf. 2 Timothy 4:11: "Mark is useful to me for ministry"). Aristarchus is from Thessalonica (Acts 20:4; Philemon 27:2). Demas, here a fellow worker, will later desert Paul for love of the present age (2 Timothy 4:10). Luke, the physician and author of Luke-Acts (Colossians 4:14). The list connects Philemon to the broader Pauline network.
25: Hē charis tou Kyriou Iēsou Christou meta tou pneumatos hymōn (the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit): the closing grace-benediction — the same formula as Galatians 6:18 and Philippians 4:23; the singular spirit (pneumatos) may refer to the individual spirit of Philemon as primary addressee, or the community's collective spirit. The entire letter has operated on the logic of grace: the grace that saved Onesimus (Philemon 1:10-11), the grace Paul asks Philemon to extend (Philemon 1:17-21), and the grace of the Lord that both grounds and enables the human grace Paul requests. The benediction does not merely close the letter — it names the power that makes the appeal possible.
